metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (or explicitly note in the plan) whether WS1-3 adds a reconcile call to MCP server startup; if not, remove MCP startup from the 'entry points that reconcile under the write lock' claim, or add a WS1-3 sub-task to wire it.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'MCP server startup' as an entry point that 'reconcile[s] unconditionally under the write lock' alongside init/re-init and fsck --repair. Reading `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` shows `build_server()` / `main()` register tools but make no call to `init_core`, `ensure_initialized`, or any reconcile path at startup. This is an empirically checkable architectural claim that the code refutes.
findings.location: What section: 'entry points (init/re-init, `rebar fsck --repair`, MCP server startup — all reconcile unconditionally under the write lock)'
findings.suggested_fix: Either confirm WS1-3 adds `ensure_initialized`/reconcile to `mcp_server.main()` and cite that workstream explicitly, or remove 'MCP server startup' from the entry-points list and replace with the accurate scope (CLI write arms + fsck --repair).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) add an explicit WS1-3 sub-task to retrofit `_migrate_gc_config` into the `ensures.py` registry with a stable id and typed result so it actually demonstrates the full pattern, or (b) clarify in the plan that gc-config is only a conceptual reference for the check-then-act logic, not a literal registry entry.
findings.finding: The plan names 'gc-config as the worked reference unit' for the ensure-pattern, asserting it demonstrates 'registry + stable id + check-then-act callable returning ok/changed/failed'. Reading `src/rebar/_commands/init.py` confirms `_migrate_gc_config` exists as a plain bespoke function — it has no registry entry, no stable id, and returns `None` (not a typed ok/changed/failed result). The plan is asserting that this function already exemplifies the full ensure-pattern contract, which the code refutes.
findings.location: What section: 'Use gc-config as the worked reference unit'; AC line 1: 'with gc-config as the worked reference'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `ensures.py` (or its equivalent) exists before authoring the cross-references, OR add an explicit sequencing note that WS4 must follow WS1-3 (and name the actual file path WS1-3 will create).
findings.finding: `ensures.py` does not exist anywhere in the repository. The plan names it as the registry file where ensure units are appended (`append to the ensures.py registry`), but no such file exists under `src/` or any subdirectory. If WS4 runs before WS1-3, the documentation will reference a non-existent symbol, producing dead cross-references.
findings.location: What section: 'append to the `ensures.py` registry with a stable id'; AC1: 'registry'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a prerequisite: 'WS4 must be authored after WS1-3 lands; the registry file path is `src/rebar/_commands/ensures.py` (or wherever WS1-3 places it).' If the path is already decided, anchor every code reference in the doc to the concrete file+symbol.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either explicitly gate WS4 on WS1-3 completion (so the marker files exist when the doc is written) or note clearly in the plan that the marker names are to-be-confirmed design decisions from WS1-3.
findings.finding: The `.ensure-applied` and `.ensure-hinted` marker files named throughout the plan do not exist in the repository. The plan describes their semantics in detail (the `marker-gates-hint-never-repair` invariant, rate-limited pending-hint, etc.), but no such files, constants, or references exist in any source file.
findings.location: What section: 'the applied-set `.ensure-applied` marker + the `.ensure-hinted` rate-limited pending-hint'; AC2: '.ensure-applied + .ensure-hinted'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether `rebar fsck --repair` is being extended by WS1-3 to run ensures (a code change) or whether this is a new `--repair-ensures` flag; if it's extending existing `--repair`, that is a WS1-3 behavioral change that should be called out explicitly so WS4 documents the post-WS1-3 state only.
findings.finding: The plan states that `rebar fsck --repair` is an entry point that 'reconciles unconditionally under the write lock' as part of the ensure pattern. The actual `--repair` flag exists but its documented and implemented behavior is exclusively A3 snapshot remediation (retiring folded sources, rebuilding snapshots) — there is no ensures reconciliation in `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py`. This is a behavioral claim about an existing symbol that is not grounded in the current codebase.
findings.location: What section: 'entry points (init/re-init, `rebar fsck --repair`, MCP server startup — all reconcile unconditionally under the write lock)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `ok/changed/failed` result enum's per-value semantics (what each value means, what the reconciler does upon receiving each) in the plan before the doc author can write the AC1 'typed result' section.
findings.finding: The plan requires documenting a typed result with three values ('ok/changed/failed') for the check-then-act callable, but this enum's value vocabulary — what each of the three values means semantically, and what the caller does upon receiving each — is never defined anywhere in the plan text or in the codebase (confirmed by grep: no EnsureResult, no ok/changed/failed type exists in src/).
findings.location: What section ('a check-then-act callable returning ok/changed/failed'); AC item 1 ('typed result')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Result vocabulary' subsection to the What section: ok = already in target state (no action taken); changed = action was taken and state updated; failed = action attempted but encountered an error (caller logs and continues or halts). Confirm these align with WS1-3 implementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `ensures.py` registry's structure in the plan (entry format, stable id convention, callable signature, and any required metadata fields) so the doc author has a ground-truth schema to document.
findings.finding: The plan requires documenting 'append to the `ensures.py` registry' as the primary how-to, but `ensures.py` does not exist anywhere in the codebase (confirmed by grep), and the plan never specifies the registry's internal structure: what fields/keys an entry must have, how a 'stable id' is formatted, or what the callable signature is.
findings.location: What section ('append to the `ensures.py` registry with a stable id + a check-then-act callable'); AC item 1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the full conditional logic of the marker-gates-hint-never-repair invariant in the plan (enumerate the three marker states and what each permits/blocks for hints vs. repairs) so the doc author can write it accurately.
findings.finding: The 'marker-gates-hint-never-repair invariant' is a decision rule the guide must explain, but the rule's full conditional logic (when does the marker suppress a hint? under what conditions does 'never repair' apply? what happens when a marker is absent vs. present vs. corrupted?) is not specified in the plan — it is a referenced protocol without co-located semantics.
findings.location: What section ('the marker-gates-hint-never-repair invariant'); AC item 2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the actual committed-version recommendation in the plan (e.g., 'recommend pinning a store-format version in the committed config at version=1') so AC5 is verifiable against a concrete expected output.
findings.finding: The 'committed-version recommendation' that AC5 says the doc must 'capture' is never stated in the plan. The executor must independently determine and write this recommendation, meaning the AC can be satisfied by any recommendation — substantive or trivial — and there is no way to verify the recommendation is the intended one.

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a section (or note within the marker-lifecycle section) to `docs/migrations.md` that specifies what happens to `.ensure-applied` / `.ensure-hinted` marker files when an ensure unit is removed from the registry (e.g., explicitly state whether orphaned markers are benign, should be cleaned up by the removal commit, or are flagged by `fsck`).
findings.finding: The documentation spec does not include guidance on what happens to a `.ensure-applied` marker file when an ensure unit is subsequently *removed* from the `ensures.py` registry. Future authors adding and then retiring ensure units have no guidance on whether stale markers accumulate indefinitely, need manual cleanup, or are silently ignored — a leaking shared-state lifecycle.
findings.location: ## What / AC line 2 ('Documents the entry points, `.ensure-applied` + `.ensure-hinted`, the pending-hint, and the marker-gates-hint-never-repair invariant')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the exact docs-index file path (e.g., `docs/README.md`) so AC6's grep check has an unambiguous target.
findings.finding: The 'docs index' file that should receive the backlink is unnamed. The executor must guess which file is the canonical index (e.g., docs/README.md, docs/index.md, a top-level README). If they choose wrong, AC6 is satisfied on paper but the link lands in an undiscoverable location.
findings.location: Scope section / AC6: 'A link to docs/migrations.md is added from the docs index and CLAUDE.md'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the docs index' with the concrete filename, e.g., 'A link to `docs/migrations.md` is added from `docs/README.md` and `CLAUDE.md`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-rollback behavior bullet to the What section (mirroring AC4) so it is not an orphan criterion with no authoring guidance.
findings.finding: AC4 requires documenting post-rollback behavior ('old code ignores git-ignored markers; committed .gitattributes/.gitignore content persists harmlessly') but this topic is entirely absent from the What section. An executor focused on the What section could miss it, and there is no worked example or context in the plan to guide what 'harmlessly' means or what edge cases to cover.
findings.location: What section (no mention of post-rollback) vs AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'the post-rollback safety property: old code ignores git-ignored markers; committed .gitattributes/.gitignore entries persist harmlessly on rollback.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a grep-checkable anchor (e.g., a required section heading or key phrase) for each of ACs 1–5, as AC6 already does with its 'verifiable' note.
findings.finding: ACs 1–5 have no proving commands or grep anchors; only AC6 has an explicit verifiability statement. For a documentation plan, each content-coverage AC should have at least a grep pattern so CI or a reviewer can mechanically confirm coverage rather than relying on a subjective read.
findings.suggested_fix: For each AC, append a note like: 'verifiable: `grep -i "hot-path" docs/migrations.md` exits 0' or require specific H2 headings whose presence is grep-checkable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 'post-rollback behavior (old code ignores git-ignored markers; committed .gitattributes/.gitignore content persists harmlessly)' as an explicit topic in the What section's enumerated list so AC4 has a corresponding described deliverable.
findings.finding: AC4 (post-rollback behavior) is an orphan criterion: it requires the doc to cover 'post-rollback behavior (old code ignores git-ignored markers; committed .gitattributes/.gitignore content persists harmlessly)' but the 'What' section's enumerated topics omit this subject entirely, leaving the implementer with no described guidance on what to write.
findings.location: What section (topic list) vs. Acceptance Criteria AC4
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, append to the topic list: 'the post-rollback story (old versions of the code ignore the git-ignored marker files; committed .gitattributes/.gitignore lines persist harmlessly after a rollback, with no action required).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add post-rollback behavior (old code ignores git-ignored markers; `.gitattributes`/`.gitignore` content persists harmlessly) to the `What` section's list of topics so the writing guide is complete and consistent with AC 4.
findings.finding: The `What` section's enumeration of topics to cover omits post-rollback behavior, but AC item 4 explicitly requires it ('Documents the post-rollback behavior: old code ignores the git-ignored markers; committed .gitattributes/.gitignore content persists harmlessly'). An implementer following the What section as their writing guide will miss this requirement.
findings.location: What section vs. Acceptance Criteria item 4
findings.suggested_fix: In the `What` section, append: 'the post-rollback behavior (old code ignores the git-ignored `.ensure-applied`/`.ensure-hinted` markers; any committed `.gitattributes`/`.gitignore` entries persist harmlessly)' to the content enumeration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a required section outline (e.g., Overview, Adding an Ensure Unit, Entry Points & Lifecycle, Markers & Invariants, Performance Contract & Trade-offs, Post-Rollback Behavior, Future Work) to the plan so `docs/migrations.md` ships with navigable structure.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify any structural requirements (headings, TOC, or section breakdown) for `docs/migrations.md`, which is a complex multi-topic document. Without a required structure, the document risks being a dense prose wall that is hard to navigate, violating the T7 navigability sub-criterion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the documentation spec (and corresponding AC) to require that `docs/migrations.md` explicitly states whether the write lock is intra-process or inter-process (e.g., filesystem lock), and documents the expected behavior when two processes reconcile concurrently against the same working tree.
findings.finding: The documentation spec directs coverage of the write lock but does not specify that the doc should clarify whether the lock is intra-process or inter-process (e.g., a filesystem advisory lock vs. an in-memory mutex). If two long-lived MCP server processes start simultaneously against the same working tree, the scope of 'reconcile unconditionally under the write lock' is ambiguous — the docs, as specced, will not resolve this for readers.
findings.location: ## What ('entry points … all reconcile unconditionally under the write lock')

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
